card.title: The Peter Principle
card.teaser: In every hierarchy, people rise until they reach the job they're bad at — and there they stay.
card.coverImagePrompt: A surreal staircase made of office desks climbing into a cloudy sky, each desk occupied by a worker — confident at the bottom, uncertain at the top, cinematic editorial illustration

lessons.title: The best engineer becomes the worst manager
lessons.body: Your company has a brilliant engineer. Naturally, you promote her. Now she manages the team — badly. She misses deadlines, gives unclear feedback, and dreads Mondays. You just watched a textbook case of something named back in 1969: the Peter Principle. And the joke's on your org chart.

visual.imagePrompt: A hand-drawn org chart on a whiteboard with one role crossed out and a new title written above it, sharpie ink smudges

lessons.title: The principle, in one line
lessons.body: Laurence J. Peter, an education researcher, wrote: 'In a hierarchy, every employee tends to rise to their level of incompetence.' Companies promote people who do well in their current role — but the new role is a different job. Eventually someone gets promoted into a role they're bad at, and the promotions stop. That role becomes their career.

keyTerms.definition: Employees in hierarchies tend to be promoted until they reach a role they can no longer perform well
The first role in a career where someone stops excelling — and thus stops advancing

visual.imagePrompt: A diagram of a corporate ladder with arrows stopping at a middle rung, a confused figure seated there while others continue upward

lessons.title: Why it's almost inevitable
lessons.body: The problem isn't that bosses are dumb — it's that the signal for promotion (current-job excellence) is the wrong predictor for future-job success. Coding well doesn't mean managing well. Selling well doesn't mean coaching sellers well. Each promotion is a bet that one skill predicts a different one. Most of the time, it doesn't.

visual.imagePrompt: A clean infographic showing two separate skill trees — 'doing the work' and 'leading the work' — with crossed-out arrows between them

lessons.title: The research caught up
lessons.body: Peter wrote his book as satire, but economists later took the idea seriously. A 2018 study of 53,000 sales workers across 214 firms found that companies really do promote top salespeople — and those salespeople really do underperform as managers. Team sales dropped by about 6% under a recently promoted star. The satire had a spreadsheet hiding behind it.
lessons.funFact: Peter's original book spent over a year on the New York Times bestseller list — a business theory so painfully true, it became pop culture.

visual.imagePrompt: A line chart projecting rising individual performance, then a sharp drop after a promotion line, red zone highlighted

lessons.title: Fix it by unbundling 'up'
lessons.body: The cure isn't to stop promoting anyone — it's to stop treating management as the only path upward. Dual career ladders, lateral moves, and skill-matched promotions let great specialists stay great without being drafted into jobs they didn't want. A hierarchy can only work if 'up' isn't always the same direction.

quiz.question: Which sentence best captures the Peter Principle?
options: People rise to their level of incompetence
The best managers start as the best workers
Organizations always have too many bosses
quiz.explanation: Peter's key claim is that employees get promoted until they hit a job they can't do well — and then stop advancing.

quiz.question: What's the core logical flaw the principle exposes?
options: Doing one job well doesn't predict doing a different job well
Pay should be flat across all roles
quiz.explanation: Each promotion rewards past performance in a job that may require different skills than the new one.

quiz.question: The 2018 economic study of 53,000 salespeople found that…
options: Promoted top sellers made better managers than average
Promoted top sellers underperformed as managers
Sales managers always outperformed individual contributors
quiz.explanation: Teams under recently promoted top sellers saw sales decline — empirical support for Peter's satire.

sources.title: The Peter Principle: Why Things Always Go Wrong
sources.publisher: William Morrow & Co. (Laurence J. Peter & Raymond Hull)
